1.label: Question 1
1.scenario: A health and care insurance company has sold individual Income Protection (IP) products for many years. The demand for individual Private Medical Insurance (PMI) products in the market has increased significantly in recent years. The Marketing Director has proposed that an individual PMI product should be added to the insurer's product range.

i.question: Discuss the advantages and disadvantages of the Marketing Director's proposal.

Advantages: Attractive to potential customers as it meets customer needs to finance medical bills
As evidenced by the increasing demand for this product
Which may lead to higher new business sales
The increase in new business volumes could be significant if the trend of increased PMI sales continues into the future
Increased sales mean that fixed costs could be spread over more policies
This might mean that the insurer can offer cheaper terms for PMI than some competitors due to synergies with IP
It might also make IP premiums cheaper going forwards
Attractive premium rates that offers value for money to the customers could help enhance the company's image in the market
The overall expenses may reduce due to economy of scales
The lapse risk may be less due to competitive premium rates
Easy to incorporate the new product on existing platform
Wider product range provides the sales force / agents more options to offer their customers
As well as opportunity to cross-sell individual PMI policies to existing individual IP policyholders to generate more sale
Which would be welcome by the distribution channels
Wider product range may introduce diversification benefits
As a new product could enable the insurer to reduce its capital requirements through diversification of risks
Wider product range may also help increase brand awareness
It will increase company profits if it can be sold on a profitable basis
Disadvantages: Complex product which may be too difficult to understand by sales force
As well as customers
Upfront development costs could be significant
Which could include: system developments, product design, product pricing, setting up underwriting processes, setting up claims management processes, employing new staff, consultant fees etc.
The company may not be able to recover product development costs if it fails to generate enough sales
Most likely if the trend in PMI sales does not continue
Development may also cause resource strain
and capital strain if the solvency capital requirements associated with this product is onerous
Resource strain could have adverse effects on customer services
Capital strain could lead to a reduction in solvency ratio/position
Both could lead to complaints / reputational damage
Pricing risk as data and expertise may not be available with the insurer for this new product,
In particular that different expertise/approach/model will be needed for long-term (IP) business and short-term (PMI) business
One of the main risks will relate to the lack of claims data for PMI
Underwriting expertise may not be available with the insurer for PMI product
Claims management expertise may not be available with the insurer for PMI product
For example, settling claims directly with hospitals / medical providers rather than directly with the customers
There could be increased administration costs due to the completely different product features of a PMI product in comparison with an IP product
It may be difficult to break into the PMI market if competition is high from established providers or if new entrants launch first and attract be the increasing sales
Future demand may drop, e.g. if state provision improves or the economy enters a recession
Writing more products could increase operational risk for the insurer
Company has no credibility in selling PMI business as it has not done so before

ii.question: Describe how the insurer would set the assumptions for pricing the PMI product.

General: The company would need to decide the extent to which there were any margins for prudence in its assumptions
Or that the assumptions needed to be best estimates.
As this is a new product, the company is likely to rely heavily on external data sources
Mortality: Death under a PMI will not trigger a claim event, it will just cause a termination to the policy
Company's recent experience under IP may be used for healthy lives (non-claiming) and lives in claims separately
Company's recent experience under existing IP products may be used for adjustments as the company sold this products for many years.
Mortality rates should be split by age and gender.
For PMI, it may be ignored as this may be deemed less significant than other factors that give rise to benefit payment
Morbidity: This is the most significant assumptions for PMI
This will involve setting the assumption for incidence rates
As well as the expected costs of medical treatments
As the product is new to the company, external sources/assistance may be needed
For example - reinsurers, external consultants, population data, medical journals
Consider the appropriate period that the data should cover, e.g. the last 3-5 years
And consider the relevance/format/flexibility/cost of these external sources
Which will affect the level of credibility and adjustments necessary to these external data sources
Data should cover both exposure data and claims data.
Costs of treatments could be obtained directly from network of hospitals.
Past data should be adjusted to allow for any expected trends that could affect the propensity of claims and the cost of claims:
Economic factors
Medical inflation
Medical advancement
Pandemic
Differences in target market
Differences in underwriting and claims management
Differences in policy conditions, e.g. excesses / policy limits, NCDs, hospital deals preauthorisation requirements
Changes in state benefits
Morbidity data should be grouped into homogeneous segments/groups
For example – age, gender, smoker status, geographical location.
The level of granularity of data grouping should take into consideration relevance within vs credibility of the groups
Expenses: Company's recent years experience under existing IP product
And split into fixed, variable, direct, indirect, initial, acquisition, maintenance, claim investment expenses
Adjustments for expected volumes of business when considering spreading of overheads
Adjustments for additional expenses under PMI for claims processing
Product development costs might also be higher as more effort is needed to derive incidence rates, develop policy literature and train sales staff for this product
Recouping the development costs may need to be spread over a number of years to keep premiums at a reasonable level
Claims expenses for PMI are also more onerous than for a life insurance product as more time and medical information will be required to determine the validity of a claim
Expense inflation: Company's recent expense inflation experience should be analysed to determine the basis for future projection
The following may be considered when setting the value of this parameter:
Current rates of inflation, both for prices and earnings
Expected future rates of inflation implied by the investment market
The difference between the return on government fixed interest securities and on government index-linked securities, when such exists, and
Recent actual experience of the industry
Lapse / Renewal: As PMI products are generally annually renewable
Company's recent experience under existing IP business is unlikely to be relevant
Industry experience of similar products could be useful if this is available
PMI lapse rates are heavily correlated with claims experience
As generally the non-claiming/healthy members' lapse rates are significantly higher than those claiming members
This is because the unhealthy lives will find it more difficult to obtain similar insurance cover with another insurer and are more likely to value the benefits of the product
Lapse/Renewal data would be grouped by distribution channel/duration in force.
Adjustments for other factors such as: economic conditions, level of competition, distribution methods, after sales service etc.
Investment Returns: Company's experience under existing IP product may not be relevant for PMI product as the nature and term of liabilities would be different from IP products
The investment pattern for PMI product liabilities is likely to be of shorter term as compared to IP product liabilities
Investment returns assumptions for PMI benefit may be slightly lower as compared to IP benefits
For PMI, the investment return is expected to have minimal impact, and may be ignored in pricing
Profit Margin: This should be based on the company's own internal target of expected return.
The company may need to make adjustments for additional risks associated with launching a new product
Others: Cost of capital allowance should be in line with company's internal risk management policies
Need to make appropriate allowance for tax
Business mix such as case size, age, gender etc
Estimated / Expected volumes of new business

iii.question:
As the individual PMI product is a brand new product for this company, its existing IT system will need to be upgraded and modified in order for it to carry out all the required functions.

Outline the modifications that may be required to the company's existing IT system.

Pricing: Pricing process / approach for the pricing of the new product
The system will require the functionality of storing multiple premium tables as this is a yearly renewable product
The IT systems should be flexible enough to allow for inserting renewal premium tables each year while preserving the previous year premium table
The system will need to capture incident rates
As well as the expected costs of each of the key medical treatments
Substandard premium rates for extra loadings for poor health or risky occupation etc. will need to be captured
Underwriting and Claims: e.g. full medical underwriting for IP vs moratorium underwriting for PM
Need to capture all the benefit information such as: Maximum limits on per day room expenses, Excess, Deductibles, fixed benefits or indemnity basis, pre-existing conditions, waiting period
Record of claims history of the paid claims
Pre-authorisation of claims process, if any
And authorisation codes and treatment codes (which would not have been needed for IP)
IP has income benefits, whereas PMI has more 'one-off' benefits
Limits on cost of medical treatments
A key difference for PMI is that claims are paid directly to the healthcare providers / hospitals
Reinsurance: The reinsurance premium tables will need to be incorporated
Alignment of the benefits conditions and premium tables with corresponding reinsurance treaties
Management and administration of reinsurance premiums and reinsurance recoveries
Reserving / Financial Reporting: Valuation process / approach for the calculations of policy reserves / liabilities
e.g. statistical methods vs case estimates
Claims data and premium income should be stored so that the valuation data can be extracted for valuation purposes

Different reserve calculation would include Unearned Premium Reserve (UPR), Unexpired Risk Reserve (URR), Additional Unexpired Risk Reserve (AURR), Incurred But Not Reported Reserve (IBNR), Incurred But Not Enough Reported (IBNER)
Amend systems to produce required figures for financial reporting
Admin / New Business / Distribution / Ot: Records of arrangements with sales agents / distribution channels, including:
Management and administration of commission payments to sales agents / distribution channels
Management and administration of payments of premiums collected by sales agents / distribution channels to the insurer
System generated quotes / renewal letters to the policyholder ahead of each renewal date
Calculation of the renewal premium corresponding to the current age
Any other relevant adjustments e.g. modification in policy terms, claims experience etc.
PMI is more likely to incorporate experience rating such as No Claims Discount
There are different types of lapses for PMI: non-renewals and mid-term lapses, so system needs to be able to deal with that
For mid-term lapses, the system would need to be able to cope with refunds of the unexpired part of the premiums paid
PMI is more likely to offer family covers
PMI is more likely to have variants such as comprehensive vs budget cover (as it is indemnity, rather than fixed cash benefits)

2.label: Question 2
2.scenario:
Company X has in its product range an individual Critical Illness (CI) product with reviewable premiums. The product offers lump sum tiered benefits depending on the severity of illnesses for the specified critical illnesses diagnosed after an initial waiting period of 90 days.

The insurer has the following reinsurance arrangements with a reinsurance company, Company Y, for its individual CI product:

First layer of reinsurance arrangement – Surplus Treaty
The retention amount is $25,000.
The total reinsured amount in excess of the retention is subject to a maximum amount of $50,000.
The reinsurance premium rate is $1.50 per $1,000 sum reinsured.

Second layer of reinsurance arrangement – Individual Risk Excess of Loss Treaty
The stated excess point is $100,000.
The cover is to a stated upper limit of $200,000.
The reinsurance premium rate is $0.50 per $1,000 sum reinsured.

The policy and claims information relating to two policies are set out in the tables below:

Insurance policy A: Date of issue 1 January 2019, Sum insured $110,000, Insurance premium (p.a.) $330
Insurance policy B: Date of issue 15 February 2019, Sum insured $200,000, Insurance premium (p.a.) $600

The claims reported under these policies are as follows:

Policy A:
Date of claim diagnosed 3 March 2019, Lump sum $40,000
Date of claim diagnosed 12 December 2019, Lump sum $70,000

Policy B:
Date of claim diagnosed 15 August 2019, Lump sum $40,000
Date of claim diagnosed 30 January 2020, Lump sum $160,000

i.question:
Calculate:

(a) the reinsurance premiums

(b) reinsurance claims recoveries

under each reinsurance treaty for Policy A and Policy B.

(a) Reinsurance premium: Surplus Treaty: Sum Reinsured = $50,000; Reinsurance Premium = 1.50 x 50 = $75
XoL Treaty: Sum Reinsured = $10,000; Reinsurance Premium = 0.50 x 10 = $5
Total Reinsurance Premium for Policy A = $80
Surplus Treaty: Sum Reinsured = $50,000; Reinsurance Premium = 1.50 x 50 = $75
XoL Treaty: Sum Reinsured = $100,000; Reinsurance Premium = 0.50 x 100 = $50
Total Reinsurance Premium for Policy B = $125
(b) Reinsurance recoveries: Claims of $40,000 under policy A is not payable as it falls within waiting period
Alternatively, it is also acceptable to assume that any further claims (i.e. the $70,000) is not payable under policy A as the policyholder has breached the policy's terms and conditions
Only the second claim ($70,000) is valid because the first claim falls within the waiting period
Total Reinsurance Recovery for Policy A = $45,000
Policy B: Sum Insured $200,000; Total Claims = $40,000 + $160,000 = $200,000
XoL Treaty Recovery [assuming treaties operate in parallel] = Min (100,000, 200,000 - 100,000) = $100,000
Or XoL Treaty Recovery [assuming XoL only operates after Surplus cover exhausted] = Min (50,000, 200,000 – 100,000) = $50,000
Total Reinsurance Recovery for Policy B = $150,000 [parallel] or $100,000 [sequential]
Alternative solution (where the surplus proportion is used, i.e. proportion reinsured = Sum Reinsured / Sum Assured):
Policy A Surplus Treaty Recovery = Min (50,000, 50,000/110,000 x 70,000) = $31,818
Policy B calculations remain the same as Solution 1

ii.question: Discuss the suitability of the existing reinsurance arrangements for Company X's individual CI product.

Suitability of reinsurance arrangements: The proposed reinsurance arrangements would provide a number of general benefits such as:
Enable the insurer to limit its exposure for policies concerned
Avoidance of large single losses
Enables the insurer to smooth its financial results
Availability of expertise from reinsurers, such as experience data, pricing, underwriting, claims management
Both covers enable insurer to write larger risks, which might otherwise be beyond its writing capacity
The XoL treaty limits company X's exposure to large individual claims
The combination of both reinsurance treaties will allow company X to use its available capital more efficiently
The reinsurance premium rates appear to be relatively lower than the insurer's premium rates

Which could be a result of the reinsurers passing the benefits of regulatory/tax arbitrage (if any) to the insurer. E.g. reinsurers may be subject to lower regulatory capital requirements and/or lower tax rates than insurers in the country under question
In Risk XoL, reinsurer agrees to indemnify the cedent for the amount of loss above $100,000 subject to an upper limit of $200,000
Risk XoL treaty is suitable for policies with Sum Insured above $100,000
Maximum Sum Insured of $300,000 is covered under these layers of reinsurance treaties
The insurer is at risk for writing policies beyond Sum Insured $300,000
The level of risk to which the insurer is exposed would depend on how many policies have a sum insured beyond $300,000
The insurer's retention limit is $25,000 for policies with Sum Insured below $75,000
The insurer is exposed to an additional risk maximum up to $25,000 under policies with Sum Insured beyond $75,000
i.e. there is a gap between the reinsurance layers.
The flat reinsurance rates for surplus treaty may be expensive if average age of the portfolio is lower than the average age expected in reinsurance rate calculation
The two layer of treaty are likely to be beneficial to the insurer
As the Risk XoL reinsurance rates is cheaper than surplus treaty rates
Two layers of reinsurance treaty is complex to administrate
Which could add to the company's maintenance costs
Both reinsurance arrangements are written under treaties, which provide certainty of coverage for the insurer
There are no details of indexation of limits (i.e. a stability clause), which might reduce the suitability of the reinsurance arrangements over time
Suitability could further be assessed by comparing reinsurance claims with reinsurance premium paid over a period of time as a measure of value of reinsurance
If the policyholder makes a series of claims under their tier policy that are below the retention level (i.e. $25,000), then the insurer will not recover anything from the reinsurer

3.label: Question 3
3.scenario: Country X is a developing country. The government of Country X has noted that while there is high demand for life insurance and general insurance products, there is very low demand for health and care insurance products. As a result, most insurers in the country do not currently offer any health and care insurance products.

i.question: Suggest possible reasons for the low demand for health and care insurance products in Country X.

Reasons for low demand: There is no requirement for individuals and corporates to have insurance in place
Employers are not required to have group insurance schemes for employees

As a result of the Government requiring employers to provide other forms of insurance like death benefits and pension for employees, the employers are reluctant to incur further costs to provide other insurance benefits
The state may already provide for all health and care needs of the nation through following possible mechanisms:
Citizens have access to free health care (hospitals, pharmacies etc)
State health facilities are of high quality hence no need for individuals to seek to avoid waiting lists
Or seek to have access to private rooms etc
There might be a lack of need for private insurance products

IP – if there is a lack of paid employment (with many individuals being housepersons, in informal work or unemployed), there is less likely to be a financial loss (or possibility of being able to prove the loss of income) if these individuals are unable to carry out paid employment
CI – lower life expectancies in developing countries mean that life cover is more important than health cover
And/or lower life expectancies make many CIs less prevalent (e.g. fewer age-related cancers, dementia etc.)
LTCI – fewer people likely to reach old ages if life expectancy is low in developing countries
Family members more likely to provide care for those who do reach old age
And a likely lack of private care homes in the country (see PMI below) means that there is no reason to buy LTCI
PMI – there is likely to be a lack of private hospitals (even if private hospitals exist in big cities, there is likely to be a large rural population without access), so no reason to buy PMI
There may be lack of consumer trust in the health and care insurance sector due to previous mis-selling
Intermediaries / Distributors may be reluctant to sell health and care products as other forms of insurance are easier to sell
Or they do not feel that the efforts involved in selling health and care products are supported by the financial incentives / commission levels
There may be cultural barriers with greater consumer focus on non-traditional forms of medicine which are not covered under the typical health and care products
Income and Affordability: Citizens have low incomes and hence cannot afford to pay for health and care insurance
Health and care insurance products are considered as a luxury and are therefore not a priority for citizens when compared to other types of insurance such as motor property or life cover
Health and care insurance products are relatively expensive compared to other insurance products
High level of fraudulent claims, or other factors resulting in disproportionally high premiums making health and care products unaffordable to most in the population
Lack of strategic initiatives from health and care insurers to provide micro insurance products and facilitate financial inclusion
Awareness and Perceptions: Lack of awareness of the importance of health and care products by the general population (generally believed to be a state role)
Social perceptions place value on aspects like preparing for death and providing supports for dependents
Insufficient government awareness initiatives to highlight the importance of health and care insurance
Insurers' marketing strategy: Poor and ineffective advertising by health and care insurers leading to a lack of interest in the products
The health and care insurance industry has previously had scandals and reputational issues that have eroded trust in the industry and pushed customers away. For example:
High claim declinature rate has led to loss of trust by customers
Limited choice of medical providers for customers

Relatively low commission rates or remunerations on health and care products does not provide intermediaries / distribution channels the incentives to target these products to their customers actively

ii.question: Suggest the possible impacts and consequences of low demand for health and care insurance on Country X.

Insurers / Healthcare Providers: Low private health financing may compromise growth of the private health service provision standards
Low demand may mean high cost to insurers due to a lack of economies of scale
And this may also discourage innovation
There will be no incentives to build private hospitals because of low demand for private medical treatments
Doctors and other practitioners are unlikely to be remunerated adequately for providing modern health and care services
Which could have a knock-on effect on the service standards
The lack of healthcare providers could lead to monopolies and increase the overall costs of access to healthcare
If there are only limited sales possible then there is no point putting much investment into product innovation or its operations
State / Government: Without private financing, the state has to meet a significant portion of the national heath and care budget
Which could put pressures on the government's overall budget and may lead to the following consequences:
Government may have to borrow significantly or print money to meet the health and care budget which could result in high inflation for the country
Or adverse effects on the strength of its currency
If the government fails to meet the budget, this could lead to poor health standards in the country
And high mortality rates in the population.
For countries with a voting system, this could lead to the current government being voted out of power
Morbidity rates in general are likely to become higher than acceptable
Which could lead to increase in waiting lists for national health services
And increased pressure on national health services
Lower life expectancy will reduce the working and productive life of individuals
This will result in lower productivity for the economy
As a healthy nation generally drives economic growth for the nation
This could also affect investment opportunity for the country as it is less likely to be a preferred investment destination for foreign investors
LTCI – an increase in the burden on the state if it provides a basic level of care
LTCI – an increase in the burden on families, reducing their ability to work, so reducing economic output
PMI – large numbers of individuals with untreated injuries / ailments (e.g. cataracts) with a subsequent impact on their ability to work (and hence economic output)
Citizens / Policyholders: Low demand could lead to lack of choice / competition
So citizens could end up paying more for private health and care insurance than they should
As citizens need to meet health costs out of their own pocket, this would reduce their disposable income in general.
Serious illnesses and incapacity without health and care insurance could force citizens into financial hardship
This could result in many families being trapped in a poverty cycle and not able to accumulate wealth and pass to the next generation
IP – issues for individuals if they are injured, so unable to work and support their families
IP – issues for companies if they provide sick pay to employees, but with no insurance provision to cover it (reduced profits, resource issues)
CI – higher mortality rates due to adequate CI treatments not being affordable
CI – increase in financial hardship and poverty if individuals unable to finance the lifestyle changes needed caused by a critical illness

iii.question:
The government has decided to promote health and care insurance products through various initiatives, with the objective of increasing demand for these products. These initiatives include the following:

All employers will be required to provide basic Private Medical Insurance (PMI) cover for their employees.
Premiums paid for Critical Illness (CI) and Income Protection (IP) products will be tax exempt going forward.
Insurance companies will pay a lower corporation tax rate on profits generated from their health and care products.
Service providers of private medical services will have a 20% subsidy, paid by the government, on the fees charged to their customers.

Discuss other initiatives, in addition to the four mentioned above, that the government could consider in order to meet its objective.

Other government initiatives: The state may be able to exclude certain segments of the population from certain aspects of the national welfare scheme
Or reduce the scope of provisions under the national welfare scheme
The government could require employed individuals to take out private health and care insurance cover on a mandatory basis
The government may introduce a Means Test
Where individuals with income above a given threshold may not have free access to state health facilities
And may be required to have health and care insurance cover on their own
The state can also offer a limited package of health services for free and require the citizens to make private provisions on additional health services
The state can reduce the cost of private purchase of health care services by directly subsidising providers of insurance
The government can top-up the premiums for each individual so that citizens are able to afford augmented and adequate levels of cover
The government can meet part of the claims cost per claim and ensure better payouts are given to citizens to provide value for money over premiums paid
The government may also provide special grants to insurers for them to meet e.g. expenses and other costs of providing insurance services
However, subsidies increase expenditure for the government which can add to the budgetary pressures
Depending on the type of subsidy, some subsidies may have poor structural design which could result in not meeting their intended objectives
The state can offer a general reduction in tax for health and care insurance policyholders
The state can carry out national awareness campaigns on the importance of health and care insurance and how citizens will benefit from it
The campaigns can include testimonials from individuals who benefited from having health and care insurance cover
The government could conduct survey and gather feedback from citizens on the key reasons why they opted not to take out health and care insurance
Which could help the government to decide what initiatives to put in place to encourage its citizens to seek cover
For example, if it is due to a general mistrust towards insurers, the government may then proceed to put in place stricter regulations to provide better policyholder protection
Mandatory requirement for employers to provide PMI cover for their employees could be extended to other products (IP, CI)
The government could extend the tax-reliefs / subsidies to LTCI and PMI
e.g. no tax on investment returns could be very beneficial for LTCI
The tax-reliefs / subsidies could also be extended to life and general insurers that sell health and care insurance as riders to their existing policies
Tax incentives could be provided through taking premiums from gross of tax income
Or benefits payments are exempt from income tax
The government could itself 'sell' health and care insurance products (underwritten by private insurers if necessary) to increase confidence in them
The government could buy up the unused services of private health and care providers in order to ensure their operations are profitable and to help keep their costs down
In return for the subsidies the insurers are getting, the benefits could be passed to policyholders through price caps which could encourage sales
Government could enforce minimum standards e.g. consistent wording in policyholder literature to increase consumer confidence

iv.question:
Company A is an insurance company in Country X, which writes both individual and group CI policies.

Set out the strategies that Company A may implement to take advantage of the government's initiatives.

Statutory requirement on employers to pr: The insurer should act quickly to be one of the first companies to launch a product to target this newly created opportunity
The design of the product should at a minimum meet the basic level of benefits under the statutory requirements
The company should carry out a market research / survey to determine what the employers expect as part of an acceptable group PMI offering
The results of the market research / survey may help provide the company with further information on product design, such as
Whether employers want to have different levels of cover according to roles and seniority, e.g. executives have more comprehensive cover such as access to foreign treatment and private hospitals
The premium frequency that is expected by employers. E.g. annual, quarterly, monthly
How sensitive are employers to the level of premiums
And any indications on the maximum amounts they are willing to pay per employee
The additional benefits that the employers expect on top of the minimum benefits
The company should carry out a marketing and awareness campaign to employers promoting the benefits of the group PMI that the insurer is selling, which could include:
The potential of improvement on productivity of employees because of better and faster medical treatments for their employees
The benefits may help retain existing employees
The benefits may help attract potential employees
Enhancing the image of the company
The insurer may try to differentiate itself from competition by designing bespoken PMI product for employers in different industries
For example, mining companies may require different PMI version compared to financial services companies
The company should set up a special business development team to concentrate on developing this line of business
The company should also appoint account relationship managers who then handle key employers / accounts and help drive new business and retain existing business
The company could attempt to cross-sell the new PMI product to existing CI customers
The company could leverage its existing CI expertise by (for example) trying to sell bundled products, e.g. a combined CI / PMI product
And PMI benefits might be used to subsidise CI cover to make it more attractive
Tax Exemptions: The reduction in corporation tax for insurers will make the health and care insurance products more profitable and the insurer can pass the tax benefits to policyholders
Some or all of the tax benefits could be factored in product pricing
Which is a balance between profitability and lower premiums
Part of the tax benefits may be utilised to strengthen the company's solvency ratio
Savings on corporate tax may be further invested in advertising and growing sales
The company should carry out a campaign on how individual customers can take advantage of the tax emption on premiums to increase levels of cover
And the company could ask the government to endorse its campaigns to add credibility
Provide training to sales agents / distribution channels so that they are able to explain the tax exemptions and how customers should take advantage of them
The company could try to upsell to maximise the tax benefits: For example:
Maximise sales volumes (through good marketing, promotions, etc.)
Push to sell bigger / more comprehensive policies
Write health and care products with additional (life / general insurance) rider benefits
Government subsidies to Service Provider: Negotiate with service providers to agree arrangement to take advantages of the government subsidy
The exact proportion on how to split the government subsidy between the company and its service providers will depend on the relative negotiating powers between the two parties
Part of the subsidy could be passed to customers through reduced premiums, which could help drive new business and retain existing business

4.label: Question 4
4.scenario:
Company A is a large health and care insurance company and a market leader in both individual and group Private Medical Insurance (PMI) in Country X. All policies are written on a yearly renewable basis.

A recent influenza pandemic has severely affected many countries, including Country X. One key consequence of this pandemic is that maternal clinics and hospitals in Country X now require expectant mothers to have an influenza test before being admitted for child delivery. The influenza test is only available from a small number of medical laboratories and attracts a significant fee.

The Marketing Director of Company A has proposed that all future PMI policies written over at least the next 12 months should cover the cost of the influenza test without an increase in premiums.

Policy Wordings vs Customers' Reasonable: There is need to look at the existing policy wordings to see if they explicitly and clearly exclude illnesses and treatments related to the influenza pandemic
If influenza pandemic is not explicitly excluded in the policy terms and conditions, then the insurer has to honour the claims as usual
Even if the wordings exclude the costs, customers may not be made aware of its exclusion at the point of sales
So some policyholders may still consider that it is reasonable to expect the insurer to pay for treatment costs relating to the influenza
Declining the claims may lead to customer complaints
And potential court cases and additional legal costs
Which could escalate to significant reputational damage for the insurer which can go beyond the period of the pandemic
If the court cases turn out to be in favour of the complainants, this could set the precedent of further claims
This may also lead to a significant fine / financial penalty by the regulator
Significant rise in litigation and scrutiny from the regulator might occur and the insurer will incur additional costs to meet regulator's requests
Reputational damage may affect new business volumes and reduce sales compared against the budget and targets
This could lead to lower profitability through inability to spread fixed overheads and potential increase in non-renewals
The company may want to carry out a market survey to assess the customers' expectations around the pandemic-induced claims before deciding on the way forward
Competitors in the market: If the company's competitors are not prepared to provide this benefit, the insurer can differentiate themselves by providing the benefit
If competitors are already covering the benefit, the insurer may need to match its competitors to remain competitive
Some market intelligence may need to be obtained to help determine market practice
And how those that have covered the benefit are performing in terms of claims experience
Market intelligence could be sought from reinsurers and actuarial consultants
Distributors might like this and use it to promote the company's products which should help to increase sales
Marketability: Covering the benefit is likely to make the product more marketable especially to potential new business which should help to increase sales
Claims experience for new business may not be significantly higher since new female clients will have a 9 month waiting period to cover pregnancy related costs

Claims experience for new business might also not be significantly higher because the 'flu pandemic might be short-lived, so by the time women go into labour (in 9 months+), a significant proportion of the population may have been vaccinated
Existing clients may have to wait until renewal to obtain the offer, so would be unhappy and dissatisfied
Which could lead to lapse and re-entry
Existing clients are however more likely to renew their policies
And may also refer the insurer to their friends and family
This will lead to increased business volumes and boost revenue for the insurer
When the pandemic eventually ends, the insurer is likely to gain a positive brand image which can further improve its ability to generate premiums
The proposal should apply for both individual and group business, which will make it fair and consistent to the company's customers
Cost of Benefit: The cost of providing the benefit needs to be considered before extending the benefit
There are very few medical laboratories which may lead to excessive prices due to lack of supply amid high demand
This would make the costs for providing free benefit significantly high and affect the profitability of the product
In order to manage the costs, the insurer could approach one of the medical laboratories and put in place a preferred servicer provider agreement
This can help lower the cost of benefit as the laboratory will be guaranteed of higher volumes of business and can provide discounts to the insurer
As the benefit is provided to a very specific segment of the policyholders, the overall total costs may not be significantly high which could can be sustained by the insurer
This is because expectant mothers at any particular time, if the lives in the book are well diversified, may constitute a relatively small proportion of the book
The cost might not be too high as the government might scrap the requirement for the test once 'flu cases come down
On the other hand, the costs could be very high if multiple tests are required, e.g. if the baby is overdue
Costs are likely to be hard to estimate as the insurer lacks past experience for this specific cost
Sources of financing the costs of provid: Increasing the benefits offered will increase the financing requirements for the product,
As well as increasing the riskiness of the cover, so increasing margins within the reserves.
If the insurer has already set aside an equalisation or catastrophe reserve, this could be used to fund the cost without significantly altering profitability
If the overall cost is not materiality high, it could even be funded as part of the company's corporate social responsibility or marketing budget
Other reserves such as Additional Unexpired Risk Reserve could also be used to fund the cost.
Alternatively, free assets could be utilised if the level of free assets / solvency ratio is high
Reinsurance: The insurer may already have in place relevant reinsurance covers for pandemic / catastrophic events
If the cover is not explicit in the reinsurance treaty terms, the company should consult the reinsurers to negotiate what supports they are able to provide
Regulations: Providing the benefit may be considered by the regulator as what a socially responsible insurer should do,
Even though it is not yet a statutory requirement for the insurers to provide such benefit
The regulator may have already make similar recommended in the past through consultation papers
In which case the proposal would comply with the regulator's recommendations
The proposal could lead to concerns from the regulator in relation to its potential implications on the insurer's future solvency / capital position due to the uncertainty of its potential costs
Operational considerations: The company needs to ensure that the system could cope with this new benefit,
e.g. the insurer would need to set up new treatment codes.
Consider the additional expenses that will be incurred, such as:
The need to communicate the new benefit
Costs of liaising with the medical laboratories (which may be independent of existing providers used).
It will need to be confirmed what 'the next 12 months' means – e.g. what date does it actually start and finish
Consideration will need to be made to what happens to pipeline cases
Intermediaries / Distributors will need to be informed
The operational changes could be significant and costly
Practical considerations: The lack of accessibility of the 'small number of medical laboratories', which could lead to negative publicity if the insurer publicises this as a benefit
The possibility that this test will become more widely accessible in the future
And/or provided by the state in the future (in which case individuals will not need to claim privately)
As the flu test is only available to a small number of medical laboratories, the increase in demand could push the price of flu test up, adding further costs to the insurer

ii.question:
The government of Country X is proposing to require expectant mothers tested positive for influenza, to deliver in a special isolation ward. This is expected to increase the medical bill by approximately 200%. Company A holds an Unearned Premium Reserve (UPR) but currently considers the premium basis is adequate to meet future claims and expenses that it is not necessary to hold an Unexpired Risk Reserve (URR).

Suggest the possible circumstances under which holding an URR is appropriate.

Circumstances for holding a URR: URR is primarily set when it is expected the future claims and expenses till the end of the policy term will be more than the premium or unexpired premium circumstances like those might be:
Occurrence of a catastrophic event (e.g. an explosion that leads to an accumulation of injuries needing treatment)

Occurrence of sudden societal changes, e.g. a sudden increase in sports participation, possibly due to an event like the Olympics inspiring everyone! (leading to a sudden increase in sports related injuries)
Occurrence of sudden behavioural changes, e.g. a new brand of footwear that everyone buys, that subsequently leads to an accumulation of injuries
Outbreak of a particular disease in the country (endemic) e.g. some developing countries frequently have disease outbreak like cholera, typhoid, malaria etc.

The particular contract was deliberately under-priced to achieve a particular business goal e.g. to retain corporate clients who would otherwise move to another insurer or to penetrate a particular market
The business mix in a particular period might significantly change and indicate that claims experience will be higher than expected
This could occur where more high risk lives were onboarded than expected
Interim experience monitoring may indicate that the product was significantly under-priced hence need to set the reserve till the next premium review
This may have been due random fluctuations which may not necessarily indicate incorrect pricing
Inspection by the regulator or auditors might result in the regulator requesting an URR to be set aside
Premiums (and hence the UPR) should already include margins, so a URR will only be needed if these margins are insufficient to cover the financial losses caused by the circumstances mentioned above
A decline in the quality of healthcare services provided by the state could lead to higher number of policyholders seeking healthcare services privately.

iii.question: Describe how Company A may determine the URR to address the government's proposal.

Determine URR: Adopt a prospective approach to determine how much is needed now to cover the expected claims and expenses from the unexpired risk period
The future expected loss ratio / projected claims of the business will be estimated and applied to the proportion of the unexpired premium
This will involve the analysis of past loss experience without the influenza pandemic and projecting it forward allowing for:
The expected increase in claims because of the influenza pandemic
Other trends that aren't induced by the influenza pandemic
Changes in the mix of business
Additional data and information that will influence the probability of having the flu will also be needed, in particular for pregnant women
For the expected increase in claims due to the influenza pandemic, there will be need to estimate:
Probability of an expecting mother returning a positive influenza test
The average claim cost per expectant mother (who had a positive influenza test) from admission into hospital till discharge after delivery
In order to estimate the total additional claims cost, information on the exposure will also be needed, which would include:
The number of mothers who are expecting
And the locations of these women as hospital costs might depend on geographical area
Aggregate total claims expected over the estimated period of cover (till the next premium review)
Calculate the UPR: Calculate the unearned premium reserve for all running policies,
For each policy, calculate the percentage (m%) of time that is still outstanding till the end of the policy term
UPR per policy is determined as (m% x Premiums paid)
Total UPR for the portfolio is the sum of the UPR for each policy
Calculate the additional reserve: If the expected future aggregate claims are higher than the UPR, then the difference will be set aside as URR
If the expected future aggregate claims are less than the UPR, no URR will be needed
All the calculations can be done on a per policy basis or using model points

5.label: Question 5
5.scenario:
Company A is a large health and care insurance company that currently operates in Country X and specialises in Income Protection (IP) products. The Marketing Director of Company A has made a proposal to launch an IP product in a neighbouring country, Country Y.

The Marketing Director has noted the following features relating to the insurance market in Country Y:

Employers in Country Y have a strong appetite to provide IP benefits to their employees.
Existing insurers in Country Y mainly focus on Critical Illness (CI) and Private Medical Insurance (PMI) products.
In order to sell insurance products in Country Y, an insurer will need to be authorised by the regulator of Country Y.

i.question: Explain why the regulator in Country Y will want to understand the design and pricing of the insurance products under its regulation.

Regulatory objectives: The overriding reason why the regulator will want to understand the products is because it wants to protect consumers
Showing an active interest in the practices of insurers / regulated entities would help build customer confidence and trust in the regulatory framework and insurance market
The regulator will want assurance that the needs and interests of customers are considered in the design and pricing of the product
Disadvantages to customers should be minimised or eradicated where possible
The regulator will want to ensure that the pricing approach is in line with what customers would reasonably expect given the marketing material of the product
In essence, the regulator therefore ensures that customers are not misled
The regulator would also want to know that the product provides reasonable value for money in terms of premiums in relation to benefit and when compared against competition
On the other hand, it will not want the product to be so cheap that it will ultimately threaten the providers' solvency levels
The regulator will also want to ensure that the design would not cause adverse effect to solvency levels (e.g. by offering excessive guarantees)
And that meets genuine needs of customers
The regulator will want a design that minimises the risk of mis-selling

ii.question: Set out the considerations that Company A may take in designing a suitable IP product for Country Y.

Customer needs: The customers would want a product that meets their needs
And the premium charged should be affordable
With respect to the proposed IP product, employers would want to cover their employees by providing replacement income when the employee is incapacitated to do work
Designing a group product may be more ideal given the strong appetite from employers
The customers would also want peace of mind and policy wordings that are clear and unambiguous
The product design should be sustainable over time. E.g. future changes such as moving to online sales should not cause the product design to become obsolete
Competition: The market competitiveness is also an important consideration
The market for IP is currently underserved, and hence less need for product differentiation
However if company A's launch is successful this could lead to other domestic insurers in country Y starting to sell IP business i.e. increased competition
Consider the need for cross-subsidies for marketability reasons, e.g. large policies subsidising small ones, and whether the level of new business mix risk is acceptable
Distribution: Availability and needs of distributors would affect the insurer's commission structure
It will also affect the insurer's decision on which type of distribution channel to be used for the product
Company Objectives: The return on capital or profitability required by the insurer is a key consideration
As it would impact the pricing
And whether to add onerous benefit features such as guarantees
The company culture in terms of designing of products and pricing would also be important considerations
The insurer would not want the product to be too different from the ones it already sells as it could affect consistency in brand positioning
It also would not want a product design that is not in line with the company's risk appetite and policy
Operational / Practical Issues: The system and admin requirements are also key considerations
Consider whether the system used in Country X is usable in Country Y
Consider whether the system providers are able to provide technical support in Country Y
If the product is very different to the company's existing product, it may need a new system which would increase IT costs
If languages are different between the country, it would be important to consider how the system and marketing materials can be adjusted to be in the local language
Country Y may not have the available expertise (e.g. underwriters, actuaries) locally
Which could add to upfront recruitment and ongoing costs
Consider whether reinsurance is adequately available in Country Y
The product should be consistent with practices in Country Y, such as:
Social attitudes, (e.g. the intrusiveness of data collection / underwriting / claims management)
The product should tie in with state benefits provided in Country Y
The product should be designed to be tax efficient for the company and the policyholders
Regulation and Professionalism: Consider whether there are any regulatory restrictions, e.g.:
On reinsuring business to reinsurers outside Country Y
The reserving and capital requirements for operating in Country Y will need to be considered
Which could in turn affect the company's ability to provide a suitable product at affordable terms
Professional guidelines applicable to pricing need to be factored in
And these may be based on the profession body in Country Y, rather than that in Country X

iii.question:
The Marketing Director has proposed that the morbidity rates used for the pricing of the IP product for Country Y could be based on the morbidity experience of its existing IP product sold in Country X, based on the assumption that the two neighbouring countries are likely to have similar morbidity experience.

Discuss the Marketing Director's proposal.

Arguments in favour: It would save time and resources to use Company A's own existing morbidity experience
The information is also readily available and thus reducing cost of getting data
Given the two countries are neighbouring, morbidity experience may not be markedly different
Country X's morbidity experience could be a good approximation, and with suitable adjustments could be a reasonable fit for Country Y
Which could be a result of the similarities in climate, exposure to nearby regions, resources (which might dictate the industries / occupations within the countries)
Arguments against / Considerations: However, in order to derive the appropriate adjustment factors, Company A will still need to investigate any differences in risk factors and their potential implications on the relative morbidity experiences between the two countries
These risk factors are likely to include:
Group IP vs Individual IP experience
State of economy
Political situation
Standard of living
Costs of living
Demographic profile
Standard of health provision
Underwriting practices
Policy terms and conditions
Sales methods and distribution channels
Other socio and economic factors
Claims management practices
Industries / Occupations
Attitudes to claiming
Education systems
Tax regime
Given that the income protection market in Country Y is underserved, there may not be credible morbidity rates for the market
However, there are other sources of data that are available and may be considered
For example, the insurer may get morbidity rates from their reinsurer(s) who might have experience in Country Y
Company A may also seek help from external actuarial consultants in Country Y
The insurer may also be able to use population statistics for Country Y and adjust them for insured lives
Consideration will need to be taken regarding the profile of the population segments that are likely to take out an IP policy
At least they will be for the Country Y's market instead of taking rates from another country
The regulator in Country Y may also not be satisfied that an insurer operating in Country Y is pricing its products based on the morbidity experience of another market
The current morbidity experience in Country X is likely to be based on a large stable book of business, so the margin on top of best estimate assumptions is likely to be small
On the other hand, the expected experience on a new block of business is likely to be volatile, in particular in the initial years
Which means that a higher level of pricing margin on top of best estimate assumptions may be needed
Alternatively, the additional risks could be factored into the Risk Discount Rate (RDR)
Or a higher level of required "Return on Capital" (RoC) to compensate for the additional morbidity risks
In summary, it is necessary to investigate all the potential sources of differences in morbidity rates and ascertain their impacts before making a final decision

iv.question:
The morbidity experience investigation of the existing IP product was conducted 3 years ago. Company A has decided to undertake an investigation to include the latest morbidity experience.

Describe the data requirements and approach for the morbidity experience investigation.

Data requirements and approach: The source of data is a key consideration, as discussed in detail in part (iii) above
It is important to use data that is relevant, credible and stable
The data should be sufficient to be credible
But not too old to be relevant
An investigation period covering the past 3 to 5 years is generally acceptable
Types of data required include policy data, claims data, withdrawals data etc
Data checks should be carried out for validation and reasonability
and remove any incorrect or irrelevant data
Validate data against external sources, e.g. reinsurer / market / population data
Next stage is to subdivide data into subsets that are relevant
For example: type of contract, age, gender, duration in force, smoker/ non-smoker status industry (especially for group rates), occupation, location, deferred period
The subsets should be homogenous but still be credible
Morbidity rates split: Morbidity rates would be split into:
Recovery rates whilst in claim rates
Incident year (so trends can be identified / investigated)
The morbidity rates can be determined using an exposed to risk approach
Policies data is used to calculate the time for which each live was exposed to the risk of sickness, transition to claim or recovery
The principle of correspondence is important to apply
Lives are only included in a particular age cohort if had they fallen sick on that particular date, they would have been included as a claim or recovery
This is based on the age definition e.g. age last birthday or age next birthday
For data relating to recent periods, the company would need to make appropriate allowance for incurred but not reported claims in its claims data
Compare actual morbidity rates to those that were expected i.e. an A vs E analysis
Adjustments may need to be made if there have been changes over the period of the investigation
e.g. in respect of target market, underwriting and claims management, policy terms and conditions, the economic environment, state benefits, attitudes to claiming

6.label: Question 6
6.scenario: A health and care insurance company specialising in Stand-alone and Accelerated Critical Illness (CI) products on both a group and an individual basis is in the process of preparing for its year end statutory valuation.

i.question: List the information that the insurer may need for the calculation of statutory reserves for its individual CI products.

Policy information: Benefit structure (i.e. fixed lump sum or tiered benefits)
If premiums are reviewable, information on review dates
Information on extra premiums / policy loading, if any
Information on any rider benefits / terminal illness / TPD covers
And additional premiums for riders, if any
Policy start date / Risk commencement date
Benefit cover information, e.g. which CIs are covered
If unit-linked, information on charges, e.g. Annual Management Charges (AMC)
Valuation assumptions: Discount rates / Valuation rates of interest
Claims delay information for estimating Incurred But Not Reported (IBNR) reserves
Option take-up rate and demographic assumptions for estimating Options reserves
For claims that have been reported but not yet settled, the declinature rates

ii.question: List the additional information that the insurer may need for the calculation of statutory reserves for its group CI products.

Policy information: Whether the cover extends to dependents
Scheme information: Industry in which the company is operating
Split of employees by seniority levels
Whether membership is compulsory or voluntary
And if voluntary, what the take-up rates are

iii.question:
The insurer uses a large number of computer applications, such as spreadsheets, for the calculations and consolidation of its valuation results.

Outline the requirements that the insurer may put in place over the governance and controls of these computer applications.

Basic documentation: The purpose of the computer application should be documented
The filename used should be logical and relevant to the purpose of the computer application
The file path and location in which the computer application stored should be documented
Input Validation: There should be controls in place to validate data input
There should be documentation in place to describe the operation of the control
There should be separate responsibilities for doers and checkers
And there should be evidence of checks and sign-offs
Where applicable, there should be materiality thresholds to trigger further investigation on exceptions
Separate controls are needed for manual data entry and automated data entry
Where applicable, build in controls to check input totals
And reconcile data entry back to source system
Either through built in function or manual checks back to source
There should also be evidence of actioning reconciliation differences / review of reconciliation
Output Validation: There should be controls in place to validate output results
There should be documentation in place to explain what output data is produced
What the controls are to ensure the output data is complete and accurate
There should be separate responsibilities for doers and checkers
And there should be evidence of checks and sign-offs
Where applicable, there should be materiality thresholds to trigger further investigation on exceptions
If applicable, there should be evidence of investigation of variances from calculated outputs versus prior period results
For complex and material calculations, it may also require independent review of results (e.g. using simplified version of model to validate)
An analysis of change (AoC) from one month to the next
Process & Technical Specifications docum: Computer application documentation should include operational instructions / process documentation
Explaining how to operate the computer application, what the computer application does and how it does it
For computer application with complex calculations and functionalities, technical specifications documentation should be in place
The documentation should be reviewed and signed by an independent Subject Matter Expert (SME)
Access Controls / Security: The computer application should be stored in a secure location
Which should be a central location
That could only be accessed by the designated users
If password is used, it should meet the company's minimum risk management / security requirements
There should be back-ups of the computer application
Data Integrity: Input data, calculations and output data should ideally be clearly separated (on separate tabs in the computer application
Formulae used in calculation steps should be locked and protected
The company may use colour coding or other equivalent methods to visibly separate inputs, outputs, calculations, checks
Change Controls: Any changes made to the computer application should be documented in a change log
The change log should describe what the changes are
And evidence of checks and sign-off by an independent reviewer / checker
And retaining a history of previous versions
